coredata.dc:title: N and P co-functionalized three-dimensional porous carbon networks as efficient metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Hao
Wang, Yanqiu
Hao, Jiayu
Liu, Yisi
Li, Wenzhang
Li, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Developing low-cost and high-performance metal-free oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts for fuel cells is highly desirable but still full of challenges. In this study, nitrogen and phosphorus co-functionalized three-dimensional (3D) porous carbon networks (NPCN) have been prepared by pyrolysis the zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a supermolecular gel of self-assembled melamine and amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP). The resultant NPCN catalysts possess unique 3D networks-like porous architecture, large specific surface area (743 m2 g−1) and abundant edge defects. As a catalyst for ORR, the optimized NPCN-900 (pyrolyzed at 900 °C) displays positive onset potential at 0.92 V and 0.74 V (vs. RHE) in alkaline and acidic media respectively, which are roughly close to those of Pt/C (0.93 V and 0.80 V). Additionally, the NPCN-900 exhibits longer-term stability and strong endurance to methanol over a wide pH range of aqueous media, which is much superior to that of Pt/C. Considering the outstanding activity of NPCN-900, it can be worked as a prospective metal-free catalyst to substitute commercial Pt/C for ORR in fuel cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of NPCN. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) SEM images of PCFs; (b, c) SEM, (d, e) TEM and (f) HRTEM of NPCN-900; (g) STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of NPCN-900. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution of PCFs, NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000; (c) XRD pattern of the PCFs, NC, NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000; (d) Raman spectra of the PCFs, NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) The wide XPS survey spectra of NC and NPCN-900; High resolution N 1s spectra of (b) NC and (c) NPCN-900; (d) P 2p spectra of NPCN-900. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Nyquist plots of PCFs, NC, NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000 catalysts; the inset of corresponding equivalent circuit diagram. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The i-t chronoamperometric curves of NPCN-900 and Pt/C catalysts at −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte; (b) Methanol crossover tolerance test of NPCN-900 and Pt/C conducted by chronoamperometric response in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The ORR performance parameters of different samples tested in alkaline media.
The ORR performance parameters of different samples tested in acidic media.

head.ce:title: N and P co-functionalized three-dimensional porous carbon networks as efficient metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing low-cost and high-performance metal-free oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts for fuel cells is highly desirable but still full of challenges. In this study, nitrogen and phosphorus co-functionalized three-dimensional (3D) porous carbon networks (NPCN) have been prepared by pyrolysis the zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a supermolecular gel of self-assembled melamine and amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP). The resultant NPCN catalysts possess unique 3D networks-like porous architecture, large specific surface area (743 m2 g−1) and abundant edge defects. As a catalyst for ORR, the optimized NPCN-900 (pyrolyzed at 900 °C) displays positive onset potential at 0.92 V and 0.74 V (vs. RHE) in alkaline and acidic media respectively, which are roughly close to those of Pt/C (0.93 V and 0.80 V). Additionally, the NPCN-900 exhibits longer-term stability and strong endurance to methanol over a wide pH range of aqueous media, which is much superior to that of Pt/C. Considering the outstanding activity of NPCN-900, it can be worked as a prospective metal-free catalyst to substitute commercial Pt/C for ORR in fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Under the great pressure of energy requirement and environmental pollution, green and sustainable energy resource is a common goal for the current human society. Sustainable and environment friendly renewable energy technologies, such as fuel cells and metal-air batteries, have received great attention and have been extensively studied by many researchers. However, the laggardly kinetic process of ORR occurring at the cathode seriously obstructs the output power density and working efficiency of these renewable energy systems. Nowadays, the platinum (Pt)-based precious metals have been deemed as the best catalysts toward ORR [1–3]. Nevertheless, the rareness, high cost and easy poisoning of Pt based noble metal catalysts seriously obstruct the large-scale applications of these promising energy systems [4]. Accordingly, it is imperative to develop a kind of non-precious metal ORR catalysts with inexpensive, efficient, robust stability and high tolerance towards fuel cells. To this end, enormous efforts have been devoted to seek the advanced ORR catalysts to compete with the Pt-based precious metals in terms of activity and durability, such as heteroatom-doped carbon materials [5,6], carbon-supported transition metal/nitrogen (M/N-C) materials [7,8], transition metal oxides [9,10] and sulfides [11], and their hybrids [12,13] etc..

Heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene, carbon nanotube (CNT) and porous carbon etc. have been regarded as one of the most potential substitutes of Pt-based catalysts for ORR, in that they do not suffer CO poisoning and methanol cross-over effects, have high conductivity, strong durability and high cost performance [14]. Until now, nitrogen (N) [15], phosphorus (P) [16], boron (B) [17], sulfur (S) [18] and fluorine (F) [19] -doped carbon materials have been reported to show excellent activities for ORR. Specifically, N-doped carbons have revealed higher activities than other heteroatom-doped carbons, which have been confirmed by experimental researches and theoretical calculations [20,21]. Furthermore, heteroatoms co-doped and multi-doped into the graphitic structures can further improve ORR activity on account of the synergistic effects between the dopants [5,6,22]. However, most of the carbon nanomaterials exhibit an excellent ORR performance in alkaline media, but dissatisfactory activity in acidic media because of the material structure affects [23]. Thus, it is essential to explore advanced metal-free carbon catalysts with high cost performance and strong durability for ORR over a wide pH range of aqueous media.

The carbon nanomaterials, such as heteroatom doped CNT and graphene are one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) nanostructure. And these 1D and 2D carbon materials show strong direction dependent electron transfer characters and very poor out-of-plane conductivity. It is mainly because of the existence of weak vander Waals forces in the landscape orientation and the much stronger covalent bonding in the carbon flat between the layers within these 1D and 2D carbon materials [24]. For practical applications (particularly for ORR), therefore, heteroatom doped 3D porous carbon materials with a large specific surface area, large pore volumes and convenient charge transfer paths are more desirable. Recently, You et al. developed a B and N co-doped 3D network-like porous carbon for ORR by thermal annealing the precursors of agarose hydrogel [25]. Liu et al. synthesized 3D N-doped porous carbon as advanced ORR catalyst by carbonation of shrimp shell [26]. Nevertheless, various 3D hierarchically porous carbon nanomaterials were generally synthesized with the aid of external templates such as silica templates (SiO2 spheres and SBA-15) [26–28], polystyrene spheres [29,30], solid NaCl [31] and oxides templates (MgO and porous alumina) [32], which often have some shortcomings such as multi-step, high cost and unfavorable for large-scale preparation. Therefore, facile synthesis of novel metal-free carbon catalysts with unique 3D structures and excellent ORR performance over a wide pH range of aqueous media is still of great importance but full of challenges.

Herein, we report a supermolecular gel self-assemble strategy to assist prepare N and P co-functionalized 3D porous carbon networks (NPCN), simply by pyrolysis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a supermolecular gel of self-assembled melamine and ATMP. The optimized NPCN-900 not only possesses strong durability and super immunity to methanol, but also exhibits excellent activity toward ORR in a wide pH range. This work not only realizes the switch from zero-dimensional CQDs to three-dimensional heteroatoms functionalized porous carbon networks (NPCN), but also provides a new approach to obtain metal-free ORR catalysts with highly activity and superior stability for fuel cells.

The synthesis of CQDs was based on a previous procedure with some modifications [33]. Typically, 4 g NaOH was added into 20 mL acetone and vigorous stirred for 1 h, and then the reddish brown powders were obtained after the mixture placed at room temperature for 3 days without any other additional treatments. The powders were washed with concentrated HCl (37 wt.%) and deionized water several times and separated by centrifugation, and then dried at 60 °C to get CQDs.

In a typical preparation, 500 mg melamine was dissolved in 19 mL deionized water under vigorous stirring, and heated to form a transparent solution. Subsequently, 1 mL amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP, 50 wt.%) was put into the above transparent solution and churned for 5 min. Supramolecular gel formed along with the mixture naturally cooled down to ambient temperature. To prepare 3D networks-like NPCN, the obtained xerogel and 2 g CQDs (the reddish brown powders without acid treatment) were ground about 10 min to form a fine mixture. The mixtures were pyrolyzed at different temperatures (800, 900 and 1000 °C) for 1 h under argon protection at a ramp rate of 10 °C min−1. The resultant products were named as NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000, respectively. Finally, the NPCN samples were acid treated in HCl (1 M) to remove the residual alkali and ORR-nonreactive phases, and washed several times with deionized water. The final products were dried at 60 °C for further use.

For comparison, nitrogen-doped porous carbon (NC) was synthesized based on the same method, except ATMP was replaced by the same volume of concentrated HNO3 (68 wt.%). Pure porous carbon frameworks (PCFs) were prepared by direct pyrolysis CQDs at 800 °C under the same conditions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from an X-ray diffractometer (SIMENS d500) to investigate the crystal structure of each sample. The surface morphologies of products were analyzed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 230). The detailed microstructure of PCFs and NPCN-900 were analyzed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F20). N2 adsorption/desorption tests were conducted at 77 K by a gas adsorption analyzer (JW-BK132F, Beijing). The specific surface area and pore size of the samples were calculated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) models, respectively. Raman spectra of each sample were obtained from a LabRAM Hr800 confocal Raman microscopic system under an excitation laser of 532 nm. The surface elemental composition and bonding configuration of NC and NPCN-900 were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrometer (K-Alpha 1063). The UV-vis absorption spectrum of the dispersed GQDs ethanol solution was measured by UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-1801).

To prepare the catalyst ink for electrochemical analysis, 4 mg of catalytic powders were dispersed in 10 μL Nafion (5 wt.%) and 990 μL ethanol (98 vol.%) mixed solution. Subsequently, the homogeneous catalytic ink was obtained after the mixed solution sonicated for several ten-minutes. Finally, 10 μL of catalytic ink was placed on the glassy carbon RDE (5 mm, Pine). After dried at ambient temperature, the catalyst with a content of 0.2 mg cm−2 was loaded on the working electrode. As comparison, Pt/C (20 wt.%) electrode was obtained with the similar method.

To analyze the ORR activities of the as-prepared catalysts, a series of electrochemical tests (including CV, LSV, EIS and chronoamperometry) were recorded on Zahner (Zennium, Germany) electrochemical analyzer attached with a rotating disk electrode (RDE). All the ORR electrochemical tests were executed in a normal three-electrode system. Throughout the testing process, an Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl electrode was chose as a reference electrode. 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution were respectively served as electrolyte in the process of electrochemical measurements and deaerated by high purity O2 and N2 (99.99% pure) according to the concrete conditions. All the potentials in this work were converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), E(RHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.976 V.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were recorded with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) experiments were executed at 5 mV s−1 under rotating speeds varying from 400 to 1600 rpm. In the obtained LSV curves, the potential at the cross of the tangents just before and after the onset of rise current was defined as the ORR onset potential (Eonset) [34]. The Koutechy-Levich (K-L) Eqs. (1)–(3) are used to calculate the kinetic current density (Jk) and transferred electron numbers (n):
(1)
1 J
=
1
J k
+
1
B
ω
1 / 2
(2)
B = 0.2 n F
D 0
2 / 3
ν
− 1 / 6
C 0
(3)
J k
= n F k
C 0
where J and J k are the measured current density and the kinetic current density, respectively. ω is the electrode rotation speed, F is the Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1), D
0 is the diffusion coefficient of O2 (1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1 for 0.1 M KOH and 1.15 × 10−5 cm2 s−1 for 0.5 M H2SO4), ν is the kinetic viscosity (0.01 cm2 s−1), and C
0 is the concentration of O2 (1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were implemented on Zahner (Zennium, Germany) electrochemical analyzer with the frequency range of 100 KHz to 10 mHz. The applied potential and excitation amplitude were set as −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and 5 mV, respectively.

Chronoamperometry: the stability of catalysts was tested by the current-time (i-t) chronoamperometric responses at −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 12000 s. Methanol crossover test was implemented through i-t response at −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with 3 M methanol addition at 300 s.

The overall synthesis procedure of NPCN catalysts is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . In this work, melamine not only served as nitrogen source for NPCN catalysts, but also acting as skeletons to form supermolecular gel. This is because each melamine molecule possesses nine hydrogen bond sites and several unpaired electrons, which can be served as H-bond acceptor [35]. As for ATMP, it works as the pH regulator for self-assembling into supramolecular hydrogel with melamine and phosphorus source for NPCN. Under the action of H-bond and electrostatic interactions, the supermolecular gel was formed when melamine mixed with ATMP (Fig. S1). Besides, the easy preparation and productive CQDs served as the carbon source to prepare NPCN. As displayed in Fig. S2, the CQDs were evenly distributed and the size of them mainly focused on the range of 3–5 nm. The UV-vis absorption spectrum of the dispersed brown CQDs ethanol solution exhibited a distinct absorption peak at ca. 310 nm (Fig. S3), which may be assignable to the
n
- π * electronic transition occurring on the surface of CQDs [36]. The well dispersed and small sized CQDs with the connection of numerous oxygenic functional groups can be used as abundant carbon precursor as “building blocks” to self-assemble and form the cross-linked 3D porous networks structure [26]. During the pyrolysis process, the decomposition of supermolecular gel facilitated the formation of 3D porous networks structure and heteroatoms uniformly co-doping.

SEM and TEM are exploited to investigate the detailed morphologies and microstructure of the PCFs and NPCN samples. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S4a, large number of promiscuously stacked ultrathin carbon nanosheets were formed when solely pyrolyzed the CQDs at 800 °C under Ar atmosphere. The corresponding TEM image of PCFs (Fig. S4b) revealed that there are no apparent holes and voids on the flat and thin carbon nanosheets, which is in accordance with the SEM results. But surprisingly, graphene-like nanosheets with interconnected 3D networks and abundant porous structures were emerged after pyrolyzed the homogeneous mixture of CQDs and supermolecular gel at 900 °C, as shown in Fig. 2b and c. The SEM images of NPCN-800 and NPCN-1000 were also presented in Fig. S5. The corresponding TEM images (Fig. 2d and e) clearly confirmed the chiffon shape, wrinkled texture and graphene-like networks features for NPCN-900. Moreover, as indicated by the arrows, numerous nanopores and holes conformation were appeared on the thin carbon nanosheets (Fig. 2e and Fig. S6). The 3D carbon networks with hierarchically porous structure and large surface area could promote the transport of oxygen and electrons and provide enriched edge defects, all of which are advantageous for ORR [37]. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of NPCN-900 revealed that the lattice spacing of 0.344 nm is corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2f). The elemental mapping results (Fig. 2g) confirmed the existence and evenly distribution of C, O, N and P elements in NPCN-900. The formation of 3D porous networks structure is mainly attributed to the combined effects of the self-assembly of CQDs and the decomposition of supermolecular gel. With the temperature rapidly increases, uniformly dispersed CQDs gradually self-assembled into interconnected and disorganized carbon nanosheets. Meanwhile, the decomposition of supermolecular gel releases large amounts of gas and generates instantaneous high internal pressure, which results numerous disorganized pores formed on the graphene-like carbon nanosheets during the self-assembly process of CQDs.

The surface area and porous texture of the PCFs and NPCN samples were assessed on the basis of N2 adsorption/desorption analysis, and the related BET results are listed in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all of the above samples displayed the type IV adsorption/desorption isotherm with an obvious type-H4 hysteresis loop at P/P0 between 0.45 and 1.0, which is characteristic of mesoporous and slit-shaped pores [38]. A distinct nitrogen uptake at low relative pressure P/P0 < 0.1 was observed (Fig. 3a), suggesting the existence of many micropores in NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000. The micropores/mesoporous structures of the NPCN samples can be also confirmed by their pore size distribution curves with a peak at about 4.0 nm (Fig. 3b). According to the BET results (Table S1), we can find that the specific surface area of the NPCN-900 (743 m2 g−1) is larger than that of NPCN-1000 (652 m2 g−1), NPCN-800 (165 m2 g−1) and nearly 10 times larger than that of PCFs (76 m2 g−1). More importantly, the specific surface area of the NPCN-900 is also outperforms some other carbon materials, e.g. 3D N-doped graphene aerogel (708.5 m2 g−1) [12], N-doped carbon nanosheets (652 m2 g−1) [23], B-doped ordered mesoporous carbons (626 m2 g−1) [17], N, P-doped carbon networks (375 m2 g−1) [39] and 3D N-doped porous carbon (360.2 m2 g−1) [26]. In addition, the pore volume of NPCN-900 (0.542 cm3 g−1) is at least twice larger than that of PCFs (0.243 cm3 g−1). In combination with the results of the SEM and nitrogen adsorption, it is easy to find that the morphology and surface property of the PCFs and NPCN samples are quite different. Remarkably, the NPCN-900 shows typical 3D porous network architecture and possesses high specific surface areas and large pore volume, which are all beneficial to expose the potential active sites and rapidly transfer the ORR-relevant species.

The structure characteristics of as-prepared samples can be testified by XRD technique. As shown in Fig. 3c, two broad diffraction peaks situated at around 24° and 43°, corresponding to the graphite (002) and (101) planes, were observed in the wide-angle XRD patterns of all pyrolytic carbon products [26,40]. It is indicated that the porous carbon materials derived from CQDs possess a high degree of graphitization, which is in good accordance with the results observed from HRTEM images. Besides, the peak intensity at around 24° and 43° increased with the increasing heat-treatment temperatures, suggesting that the higher pyrolysis temperatures can enhance the graphitization degrees of these porous carbon materials. Further structural information about the above samples can be observed from Raman spectra (Fig. 3d). The ratio of the D band (1353 cm−1) and G band (1590 cm−1) is generally adopted to detect the graphitization and disorder of carbon materials [41]. Obviously, the I D/I G ratio of pyrolytic carbon products is gradually increases with pyrolysis temperature, suggesting the presence of many defects in the graphite structure due to the insertion of N and P atoms [22,40]. Generally, various defect sites introduced by heteroatom doping may contribute more catalytic active centers for high efficiency electrocatalysis [26,42].

XPS was performed to investigate the elemental compositions and bonding configurations of heteroatom functionalized porous carbon materials. Fig. 4 a distinctly displayed the elements of C, O and N both were exist in NC and NPCN-900, the corresponding atomic percentages are listed in Table S2. Besides, the XPS survey spectrum of NPCN-900 appeared an obvious P 2p peak, confirming successful N and P co-doped. Observed from the XPS N 1s spectrum of NC and NPCN-900 (Fig. 4b and c), the N types can be classified into pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (400 eV), graphitic N (401.4 eV) and pyridinic N oxide (403-406 eV), respectively [12,22]. As shown in Table S3, the total amount of pyridinic N and graphitic N account for the majority of N species in NC and NPCN-900, which are generally considered beneficial for ORR [43]. The high-resolution P 2p spectrum of NPCN-900 (Fig. 4d) showed the P-C bond at about 131.5 eV and P-O at around 133.1 eV [40]. Previous studies have reported that the formation of P-C and P-O bonds could improve the electron-deficient property of carbon atoms and be advantageous for the ORR [44].

To appraise the ORR performance of the as-prepared samples, CV measurements were first performed in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S7, characterless voltammetric currents without any obvious redox peak were shown for all the samples and Pt/C in N2-saturated electrolyte (dash lines). On the contrary, the palpable cathodic reduction peaks were observed in O2-saturated electrolyte for various catalysts (solid lines). For the PCFs, the oxygen reduction peak was appeared at 0.64 V (vs. RHE). After doped with N atoms, the reduction peak potential (E p) of NC was modest moved to positive direction. Remarkably, the E p of NPCN-900 (0.80 V vs. RHE) was more positive than that of PCFs, NC, NPCN-800 and NPCN-1000 (Table 1 ). It is suggested that the N and P co-functionalized NPCN-900 has the lowest overpotential toward ORR in alkaline media compared to other catalysts prepared in this work.

To further investigate the electrocatalytic activities of each sample toward ORR, RDE tests were executed to obtain the corresponding LSV curves. Some ORR performance parameters of each catalyst were summarized in Table 1. As shown in Fig. 5b, the dual-doped NPCN catalysts (NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000) exhibited more positive E onset and higher current density than those of NC and PCFs, which was matched well with the CV consequences. Particularly, the NPCN-900 sample displayed an E onset at 0.92 V and an E 1/2 at 0.78 V, which were close to those of Pt/C (0.93 and 0.81 V) and even outperformed some recently reported metal-free ORR catalysts (Table S4). The pronounced performance of NPCN-900 could be attributed to its unique 3D porous networks structure with multidimensional electron transfer routes and fully exposure of active centers, as well as various defect sites introduced by the doping of N and P into the graphitic framework (the detailed discussion see below) [40].

Moreover, the RDE measurements with various rotating velocities from 400 to 1600 rpm were implemented to explore the ORR kinetics of the NPCN catalysts. A series of LSV curves and Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots of NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000 were shown in Fig. S8. Owning to the shortened diffusion distance at high velocity, the current density augments with increasing rotation speeds. The linear K-L plots of the above catalysts at different potentials from 0.20 to 0.70 V (vs. RHE) reflected a first-order reaction with respect to the dissolved O2. The transferred electron number (n) and kinetic current density (J
k ) of above catalysts can be obtained from the corresponding K-L plot. As displayed in Fig. 5c, the value of n is 3.89 at 0.60 V (vs. RHE), suggesting that the NPCN-900 is more inclined to choose an apparent 4e− pathway to reduce O2. Meanwhile, the corresponding J
k of NPCN-900 is 18.1 mA cm−2, which is also comparable to that of Pt/C (19.9 mA cm−2) and outperforms any other samples prepared in this work. The ORR kinetics for various catalysts was also investigated by drawing their Tafel plots, as displayed in Fig. 5d. Obviously, the Tafel slope of NPCN-900 (73 mV/decade) was smaller than that of NPCN-1000 (78 mV/decade), NPNC-800 (80 mV/decade), NC (81 mV/decade) and PCFs (103 mV/decade) and approximately equal to that of Pt/C (72 mV/decade) at low polarization region. At the low polarization region, the transfer of the first electron may be the rate-determining step in oxygen reduction. From the mechanistic point of view, a smaller Tafel slope for NPCN-900 means a quicker reaction kinetic and higher intrinsic ORR activity [12,34]. This is because that the overpotential is increases significantly with increasing current when the catalyst has a high Tafel slope [34].

Except for the outstanding performance in alkaline electrolyte, the ORR activity of NPCN-900 is also highly compete with Pt/C in acidic electrolyte. As revealed in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S9, obviously well-defined cathodic reduction peaks were observed in O2-saturated electrolyte (solid lines) for all of the samples, but a pure capacitive current without any distinct redox peak was emerged when N2-saturated (dash lines). Especially, the E p of NPCN-900 is 0.60 V (vs. RHE), heralding a decent ORR activity in acidic media. Then, LSV tests were executed on RDE to further investigate the electrocatalytic activities of each sample. The obtained ORR performance parameters of each catalyst in acidic media are presented in Table 2 . Notably, the NPG-900 displays a positive E onset of 0.74 V, which is higher than those of NPCN-1000 (0.69 V), NPCN-800 (0.61 V), NC (0.42 V) and PCFs (0.29 V), and only 60 mV worse than that of the Pt/C (Fig. 6b). Except the positive E onset, the prominent ORR activities of NPCN-900 are also confirmed by its acceptable E 1/2 and large current density (Table 2). Furthermore, the ORR activity of NPCN-900 in acidic media is also comparable and even superior to other metal-free catalysts reported in literature (Table S5).

RDE measurements were employed to research the reaction kinetics of NPCN catalysts in O2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. A set of LSV curves of NPCN-800, NPCN-900 and NPCN-1000 were presented in Fig. S10. The kinetically controlled current was increased with increasing rotation rates. The K-L plots of various NPCN catalysts and Pt/C (Fig. S11) were displayed good linearity at 0.3 V (vs. RHE), indicating efficient electron transfer process of them. Based on the K-L plots of the above catalysts at 0.3 V (vs. RHE), the calculated value of J k and n were shown in Fig. 6c. Significantly, the value of J
k (13.6 mA cm−2) and n (3.64) for NPCN-900 were comparable to those of Pt/C and larger than those of other NPCN samples prepared in this work. The small value of J
k and n for NPCN-800 may result from its low content of heteroatoms (N, P) doped in carbons and small specific surface area, which are consistent with the conclusion obtained from Raman spectra and BET results. Nevertheless, the increasing of pyrolysis temperature could also result in the massive loss of doped heteroatoms, thus reducing part of active sites of NPCN-1000. Moreover, the slightly decreased specific surface area may be another important reason for the dissatisfactory performance of NPCN-1000. The superior ORR performance of NPCN-900 was also confirmed by its Tafel plots (Fig. 6d). Obviously, the Tafel slope of NPCN-900 (143 mV/decade) is smaller than those of NPCN-1000 (174 mV/decade), NPCN-800 (179 mV/decade), NC (241 mV/decade) and PCFs (513 mV/decade). The smaller Tafel slope means the higher intrinsic catalytic activity for ORR, which is indicating that NPCN-900 has a faster reaction kinetic in acid media [12,34].

As far as we know, the ORR activity of the majority of heteroatom-doped carbon materials is still much worse than that of Pt-based catalyst, especially in acidic electrolyte [24]. So, what is the reason that the NPCN-900 sample held such prominent ORR activity over a wide pH range in this work? According to the in-depth analysis, the superior ORR activities of NPCN-900 may be attributed to the synergetic effect of N/P co-doping and unique 3D porous graphene-like networks structure with abundant edge defects. Previous studies have found that the ORR activity of carbon materials is largely depended on the atomic charge densities and spin densities [42,45]. As for N-doped carbon, the electric neutrality of carbon atoms could be destroyed due to the insertion of N atoms into the graphitic lattice [40]. The enlarged spin density and unevenly distributed charge density of carbon atoms could facilitate the adsorption of oxygen and subsequent reduction reaction on carbon [40,46,47]. The additional P-doping into N incorporated carbon could further reinforce the asymmetric spin density of carbon atoms and endow these carbon nanomaterials with higher ORR activity [40,47]. In addition, it has been reported that the defects situated at the edge of graphitic layers were probably worked as the active sites toward ORR [42]. As displayed in Fig. S6, numerous nanopores and holes conformation can be observed on the graphene-like carbon nanosheets. The more nanopores exist, the more edge defects and higher ORR activity of NPCN-900 have. On the other hand, the 3D porous networks structure with large specific surface area is conducive to expose more of the active sites, as well as provide multidimensional electron transport pathways and facilitate the accessibility of oxygen. Thus, the ORR activity of NPCN-900 was significantly enhanced when N and P were simultaneously incorporated into the 3D porous graphene-like carbon networks.

The durability and methanol-tolerance are the major parameters to estimate the ORR performance of catalyst for its practical application. As revealed in Fig. 8 a, the durability of NPCN-900 and Pt/C were characterized by i-t chronoamperometric measurements. The relative current of NPCN-900 and Pt/C both gradually reduced over time. But the NPCN-900 revealed a slower attenuation rate than Pt/C during the measurements, indicating a longer-term stability of NPCN-900 over the Pt/C in an alkaline electrolyte. Additionally, the strong durability of NPCN-900 was proved again in acid medium, as illustrated in Fig. S12. As we know, Pt/C catalyst is easily poisoned in the presence of methanol. For comparison, the methanol-tolerance of the NPCN-900 and Pt/C catalysts were investigated via chronoamperometric responses at −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). It can be seen from Fig. 8b, slight performance degradation with high current retention (95%) was achieved with NPCN-900 after the injection of 3 M methanol into the electrolyte at 300 s, which is consistent with other heteroatom-doped carbon catalysts reported in literature [40,48]. Whereas, the ORR relative current over the commercial Pt/C catalyst decreased significantly, it could be attributed to the inherent disadvantage of Pt catalyst that has vulnerability to methanol attack. The experimental results clearly demonstrate that the NPCN-900 possesses a high resistance to methanol crossover.

In conclusion, a novel architecture of nitrogen and phosphorus co-functionalized three-dimensional porous carbon networks (NPCN) has been successfully designed and constructed by simply pyrolyzing zero-dimensional CQDs and a supermolecular gel obtained from self-assembled melamine and ATMP. As a metal-free ORR catalyst, the as-prepared NPCN-900 exhibits remarkably ORR electrocatalytic activity, longer-term stability and strong endurance to methanol over a wide pH range of aqueous media. The impressive ORR performance of NPCN-900 is largely attributed to its unique 3D networks-like porous structures, large specific surface area and abundant edge defects, as well as the homogeneously incorporated N and P. This work not only realizes the switch from zero-dimensional CQDs to three-dimensional heteroatoms functionalized porous carbon networks (NPCN), but also provides a simple, scalable and template-free approach to obtain highly activity and superior stability metal-free ORR catalysts for fuel cells. Additionally, this facile approach and carbon materials with novel structure can also be applied to other fields which need high conductivity and large specific surface area.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 51474255), Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project, China (No.2016TP1007), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (2017zzts114), Open-End Fund for the Graduate Student Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (No. 150140008), open research Fund Program of Key Laboratory of Metallogenic Prediction of Nonferrous Metals and Geological Environment Monitoring (Central South University), Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.06.043.
